content.text: Tool selection is the model's decision about which tool to call at each step of the agent loop. The model does not have access to tool implementations — it reads only the

of each tool, then picks the one that best matches its current goal. The quality of that decision is determined almost entirely by the quality of tool descriptions: a precise description leads to correct selection; a vague or overlapping one causes the model to pick the wrong tool or hesitate between two similar ones.

attrs.alt: Tool selection — how the model chooses which tool to call

content.text: How the Model Selects a Tool

At each step, the model reads the current task context and scans the tool list. The process is not a database lookup — it is a reasoning step driven by natural language matching between the task and tool descriptions:
The model sees only name, description, and schema
— not the implementation. What it reads is exactly what you write in the tool definition.
— the model first picks the tool, then reads the
to determine what arguments to supply. A good description increases selection accuracy; a good schema increases argument accuracy.
The task description in messages[0] matters
— the model matches tool descriptions against the current state of the conversation, not just the original task. A tool that is not relevant at step 1 may be the right choice at step 4.
Four Signals That Drive Correct Selection

# Signal 1: Tool name — verb-noun naming aligns with the model's planning vocabulary

# Ambiguous names force the model to rely entirely on the description
BAD_NAMES = ["get_data", "do_action", "process", "helper"]

# Verb-noun names communicate intent in the name itself
GOOD_NAMES = ["read_file", "write_file", "search_code", "run_tests", "delete_branch"]


# Signal 2: "When to use" clause — guides the model on ordering and context

BAD_WRITE_DESC = "Write a file."  # model may call this before reading first

GOOD_WRITE_DESC = (
    "Write or overwrite a file with new content. "
    "Always call read_file first to see what is currently in the file. "  # ordering hint
    "Returns the path written."
)


# Signal 3: "Alternatives excluded" clause — prevents wrong selection

BAD_SEARCH_DESC = "Search for information."  # model may use this for web search

GOOD_SEARCH_DESC = (
    "Search the local codebase for a string or pattern using ripgrep. "
    "Use this to locate symbols, function names, or file contents. "
    "Does NOT search the web or external documentation."  # explicit contrast
)


# Signal 4: Return value — the model plans future steps based on what a tool returns

BAD_LIST_DESC = "List files in a directory."

GOOD_LIST_DESC = (
    "List the files and subdirectories in a directory. "
    "Returns a JSON list of relative paths. "  # model knows it can iterate the result
    "Use this to discover what files exist before reading them."
)
Guides ordering of tool calls across steps
Prevents wrong selection when tools seem similar
Model can plan future steps based on expected output
Write the "when to use" clause for every tool that could be confused with another. The model has no way to know the intended ordering unless you state it.
parameter overrides the model's free selection on a given

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# tool_choice controls how the model selects tools on a given call.
# Three modes:

# 1. auto (default) — model chooses freely; may call tools or respond with text
response_auto = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=1024,
    tools=tools,
    tool_choice={"type": "auto"},   # default; can be omitted
    messages=messages,
)

# 2. any — model MUST call at least one tool; prevents premature end_turn
# Use when the loop requires a tool call at this step (e.g., investigation phase)
response_any = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=1024,
    tools=tools,
    tool_choice={"type": "any"},
    messages=messages,
)

# 3. tool — model MUST call this specific tool
# Use to force a specific first step (read before write, search before patch)
response_forced = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=1024,
    tools=tools,
    tool_choice={"type": "tool", "name": "read_file"},
    messages=messages,
)
# After this forced call, switch back to auto for subsequent steps
— the model chooses freely, including choosing to respond with text instead of calling a tool. Use this for most steps.
— if the loop requires a tool call at a specific step (investigation, verification),
prevents the model from short-circuiting with a text response.
tool forces a specific first step
— use to enforce read-before-write, or to ensure the model starts by examining the relevant file before attempting changes. Switch back to

When two tools in the inventory have similar or overlapping descriptions, the model picks between them inconsistently. The fix is either to make their scopes clearly distinct, or to merge them into one tool with an optional parameter:
Distinct scope, not just different names
— two tools named differently but described the same way are still ambiguous. The description must distinguish the cases, not just the name.
— two tools with nearly identical purpose produce unpredictable selection. A single tool with an optional parameter is easier for the model to use correctly.
Explicit contrast is the clearest signal
. Stating what the tool does not do removes the alternative from consideration.
Enforcing Read-Before-Write with Forced Tool Choice
A common source of agent errors is writing to a file without reading it first. Using
on the first step guarantees the model has read the file before the loop switches back to free selection:
Use forced tool choice sparingly. It bypasses the model's own planning and should only be used for steps where the order is invariant — reading before writing, or verifying before deploying.

Tool selection can be verified with integration tests that run one step and check which tool the model chose. These tests are sensitive to description changes — a wording change that causes the model to pick the wrong tool will fail immediately:

import anthropic
import pytest


def get_first_tool_call(task: str, tools: list[dict]) -> dict | None:
    """
    Run one step and return the first tool_use block if the model chose a tool.
    Returns None if the model chose to respond with text.
    """
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
        max_tokens=512,
        tools=tools,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": task}],
    )
    for block in response.content:
        if block.type == "tool_use":
            return {"name": block.name, "input": block.input}
    return None


class TestToolSelection:
    """
    Integration tests: verify that the model selects the expected tool for
    each class of task. Requires a live Anthropic API key.
    """

    def test_read_task_selects_read_file(self, tools):
        call = get_first_tool_call("Show me the contents of src/app.py.", tools)
        assert call is not None
        assert call["name"] == "read_file"
        assert "src/app.py" in call["input"].get("path", "")

    def test_search_task_selects_search_code(self, tools):
        call = get_first_tool_call("Find where LoginError is defined.", tools)
        assert call is not None
        assert call["name"] == "search_code"

    def test_forced_any_prevents_text_response(self, tools):
        """tool_choice=any forces a tool call even for conversational prompts."""
        client = anthropic.Anthropic()
        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
            max_tokens=512,
            tools=tools,
            tool_choice={"type": "any"},
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What should I do next?"}],
        )
        assert response.stop_reason == "tool_use"
        assert any(b.type == "tool_use" for b in response.content)
for a locate task, not just that the agent completed. Selection failures are the most common source of incorrect agent behaviour.
Tests are sensitive to description changes
— treat a failing selection test as a signal that a description change shifted the model's behaviour. Review the change before proceeding.
to verify that forced tool-call mode is working as expected.

Tool selection is driven by the model reading tool descriptions and matching them to its current task context. The four signals that improve selection are: verb-noun tool names, explicit "when to use" clauses, "alternatives excluded" contrasts, and stated return values. Overlapping descriptions cause unpredictable selection — either make tool scopes clearly distinct or merge tools. Use
to enforce specific first steps. Test tool selection with integration tests that assert which tool the model chose for representative task descriptions.
